message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 27
Recent Green phase: Added the high-damage enchantment clause for claims. New constants HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCH_THRESHOLD=8, HIGH_DAMAGE_REIMBURSE_RATE=0.5. New helper reimbursable(amount, item) returns amount*0.5 when (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= 8, else amount. damagePayout(damage, item) now = max(reimbursable(...) - DEDUCTIBLE, 0). New helper insuredItem(policy, itemType) finds the matching item; processClaim passes it. The dragon-material full-reimbursement clause is NOT yet implemented (next test).

IMPORTANT do NOT write experiment-done.txt — 17 todo tests remain.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design; measure APP mass / McCabe / cognitive / ESLint before and after. Note that reimbursable will soon gain the dragon clause (an else-if), so don't over-abstract now, but ensure its current shape is clean. Keep whichever scores better.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE measurements:
- **ESLint smells**: 0 (clean)
- **Tests**: 27 passing, 17 todo

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for the key functions, focusing on the recently changed `reimbursable`, `damagePayout`, `insuredItem`, and `processClaim`.

**McCabe cyclomatic complexity per function:**
- `reimbursable`: start 1, one `if` + one `??` = +2 → **McCabe 3**
- `damagePayout`: no branches (Math.max is invocation) → **McCabe 1**
- `insuredItem`: arrow in `.find` has `===`, no branch keyword → **McCabe 1**
- `processClaim`: reduce callback, no branches → **McCabe 1**
- `quotePremium`: no branches → **McCabe 1**
- `runScenario`: one `if` + one `>` comparison in `quoteCount > 0` (not a branch keyword, but `>` is a comparison not counted unless boolean operator); the `if (step.op === "quote")` = +1 → **McCabe 2**

`reimbursable` has the highest McCabe (3) and is the recently-changed function.

**APP mass for `reimbursable`:**
```
const reimbursable = (amount, item) => {
  if ((item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCH_THRESHOLD) {
    return amount * HIGH_DAMAGE_REIMBURSE_RATE;
  }
  return amount;
};
```
- Constants: 0 literals (the `0` in `?? 0` is a constant) → 1 constant
- Bindings: `amount`, `item`, `item.enchantment`, `HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCH_THRESHOLD`, `amount`, `HIGH_DAMAGE_REIMBURSE_RATE`, `amount` → ~7 bindings
- Invocations: `>=`, `*`, `??` → 3 invocations (×2 = 6)
- Conditionals: 1 `if` + 1 `??` → 2 (×4 = 8)
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Total ≈ 1 + 7 + 6 + 8 = **22**

The code is already clean — ESLint reports 0 smells, the helpers are small and well-named. Let me evaluate the specifics.

**Naming evaluation:**
- `reimbursable` — returns the reimbursable amount before deductible. The name reads as a boolean ("is reimbursable") but it returns a number. This is a naming smell. A clearer name would be `reimbursableAmount` or `coveredAmount`. This reveals intent better.
- `insuredItem`, `damagePayout`, `processClaim` — all clear.

The strongest improvement here is **Rule 2 (reveal intent)**: rename `reimbursable` to `reimbursableAmount`. The hint says it will soon gain a dragon else-if clause, so I should keep its current shape (the guard-style `if`/return) which is already extensible. Let me apply just the rename.

content.text:
All green. POST measurements confirm the rename is the right call.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring Applied**: Renamed `reimbursable` → `reimbursableAmount` (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).

**Naming evaluation drove this.** `reimbursable` reads as a boolean predicate ("is reimbursable?"), but the function returns a *number* — the covered amount before the deductible. `reimbursableAmount` makes the return shape obvious at the call site in `damagePayout`. This matters more as the function is about to gain a dragon-material else-if clause; a clear name keeps the growing dispatch readable.

I deliberately did **not** restructure the `if`/return shape. The hint says a dragon clause (another else-if) is coming next, and the current guard-style early-return is exactly the shape that extends cleanly. Over-abstracting now (e.g., a rate lookup table) would be premature with only one rule present.

**Pre/Post Deltas (function `reimbursableAmount`)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (already clean, no regressions)
- Cognitive complexity: 2 → 2 (one `if` + nesting-free; unchanged)
- APP mass: 22 → 22 (pure rename, no structural change)
- McCabe cyclomatic: 3 → 3 (one `if` + one `??`; unchanged)

**Worst-McCabe target identified**: `reimbursableAmount` (McCabe 3) was the highest-complexity function in the file — correctly the focal point. Its complexity is intrinsic to the conditional reimbursement rule and at its floor for the behavior expressed; the only available net-positive lever was clarity, not structure.

**Tests**: 27/27 passing (17 todo remain) ✅

The four structural metrics were already at their floor for the behavior present, so the only genuine improvement was intent-revealing naming — chosen per Simple Design Rule 2, which trumps complexity scores. I did not write `experiment-done.txt` (17 todo tests remain).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
